title_en: Zakat and Fasting in the Maliki School
content_en:
The Mudawwanah's chapters on zakat present the Maliki school's well-developed framework for this fundamental pillar, offering positions shaped both by hadith and by Malik's characteristic attention to Madinan practice and economic reality. Imam Malik approached zakat as simultaneously a form of worship and a social institution, and his rulings reflect both dimensions.

On the nisab (minimum threshold) for zakat on gold and silver, the Maliki school follows the well-known thresholds derived from prophetic hadith: twenty mithqals of gold and two hundred dirhams of silver. What distinguishes Maliki treatment is the careful attention to the haul — the condition that a full lunar year must elapse while the wealth remains at or above the nisab. The Mudawwanah records detailed discussion of what happens when wealth dips below the nisab during the year and then recovers, with Malik holding that the year restarts from when the wealth returns to nisab level.

Maliki zakat law has a distinctive position on the zakat of trade goods (zakat al-tijarah). Malik required that merchants calculate the value of their inventory at the end of each lunar year and pay zakat on the total value at the applicable rate. This applied regardless of whether the goods were purchased for resale or were work-in-progress. The Mudawwanah records extensive discussion of how to value mixed inventories and how to handle debts owed to the merchant versus debts the merchant owes.

On zakat al-fitr, Malik held that it is obligatory (wajib) and must be paid in the food staples consumed in that region — whether dates, barley, wheat, or other grains. A significant Maliki position is that zakat al-fitr may be paid before Eid prayer by up to two days, reflecting the documented practice in Madinah of the companions distributing it shortly before the prayer.

The fasting chapters of the Mudawwanah open with the fundamental conditions for valid sawm: the intention (niyyah) must be made each night for the following day's fast. This is one of the more demanding positions among the schools, as it requires a fresh intention before each day rather than a single intention for the entire month. Malik derived this from a reading of the relevant hadith that emphasized the nightly renewal of purpose.

Maliki law treats the question of who must fast versus who is excused with characteristic attention to hardship. The Mudawwanah records Malik's rulings on travelers — a person on a journey of the distance that permits prayer shortening may break their fast, though Malik preferred that travelers fast when travel is easy. Sick individuals may break their fast when fasting causes genuine harm, not merely discomfort.

The expiation (kaffarah) for intentionally breaking a Ramadan fast — freeing a slave, then fasting sixty consecutive days, then feeding sixty poor people — is presented in the Mudawwanah with important Maliki nuances. Malik held that the kaffarah applies only when the fast is broken by sexual intercourse, not by eating or drinking, a position that narrows the scope of this severe expiation compared to some other schools.

I'tikaf in the Maliki school requires the mosque and requires that the person intending it actually remain there continuously. The Mudawwanah discusses at length the conditions under which leaving the mosque for a necessity breaks the i'tikaf.
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa dalam Mazhab Maliki
content_id:
Bab-bab zakat dalam Al-Mudawwanah menyajikan kerangka mazhab Maliki yang berkembang baik untuk pilar fundamental ini, menawarkan posisi-posisi yang dibentuk oleh hadis dan juga perhatian khas Malik terhadap praktik Madinah dan realitas ekonomi. Imam Malik sangat teliti dalam mempertimbangkan implikasi ekonomi dari setiap keputusan zakatnya, yang mencerminkan tugasnya seumur hidup sebagai mufti yang memberikan bimbingan praktis kepada masyarakat Madinah yang nyata.

Ciri khas zakat Maliki yang paling terkenal adalah kewajiban zakat atas perhiasan yang dipakai. Tidak seperti mazhab Syafi'i yang membebaskan perhiasan yang digunakan dari zakat, Imam Malik mewajibkannya. Posisi ini direkam dengan detail yang cukup dalam Al-Mudawwanah, bersama dengan berbagai kondisi dan pengecualian yang berlaku. Posisi ini tetap dipertahankan oleh ulama Maliki berikutnya dan memiliki implikasi praktis yang signifikan bagi masyarakat.

Dalam bidang puasa, Al-Mudawwanah mencakup penentuan awal Ramadan melalui ru'yah (melihat hilal), hal-hal yang membatalkan puasa, berbagai kondisi yang memperbolehkan berbuka bagi musafir dan orang sakit, serta kafarat bagi yang melanggar puasa. Imam Malik dalam beberapa posisinya lebih ketat dari mazhab lain — misalnya dalam mensyaratkan kafarat bagi kondisi-kondisi tertentu yang mazhab lain hanya mewajibkan qadha.

Pembahasan tentang i'tikaf dalam Al-Mudawwanah juga menarik karena mencerminkan praktik i'tikaf sebagaimana dipahami dan diamalkan di Madinah pada zaman Imam Malik. Beliau sangat menekankan bahwa i'tikaf yang sah harus dilakukan di masjid jami' (masjid yang digunakan untuk salat Jumat), berbeda dari pandangan beberapa mazhab lain yang memperbolehkan i'tikaf di masjid-masjid yang lebih kecil.
